topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The reception hall buzzed with the kind of loud, celebratory energy that always made Maya feel slightly peripheral. She was nursing a lukewarm sparkling water, trying to appear engaged in a conversation with a distant acquaintance, when the music shifted. A familiar laugh, sharp and bright, cut through the din. Maya’s breath hitched. Standing near the buffet table, looking impossibly polished in a deep emerald dress, was Liam. His smile, practiced and dazzling, didn't quite reach his eyes as they swept the room and snagged on hers for a fleeting second before moving on. Maya’s hand tightened around her glass, the condensation making her palm slick. She suddenly felt a strange, internal tightening in her chest, a sudden, unwelcome clenching. She forced a small, tight smile, turning her attention fiercely to the speaker, acutely aware of the space Liam had just occupied.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who it is!" Chloe’s voice was loud, and it was the sound that pulled Alex’s attention away from the dance floor. Alex had been trying to enjoy the open space, the background chatter, but then he saw him. Across the room, leaning against a pillar, was Sarah. She was wearing a vibrant crimson, and the way she laughed at something another guest said—a specific, melodic sound Alex remembered intimately—sent a strange tremor through him. He stopped mid-sip of his cocktail. His gaze locked onto hers. A sudden dryness coated his throat. He shifted his weight, subtly angling his body away from the direct line of sight, his fingers fiddling with the rim of his glass until his knuckles were white. The noise of the party seemed to recede, leaving only a high-pitched ringing in his ears.

[story 3]

I was trying to navigate the crowd, making my way from the bar to the patio, when I bumped squarely into him. It wasn't a hard collision, just enough to make the champagne flute I was holding wobble precariously. Liam. He was standing near the entrance, deep in conversation, and when our eyes met, the air around me seemed to thicken. The noise of the wedding—the clinking glasses, the excited chatter—all seemed to mute themselves into a dull roar. I could feel a distinct pressure building behind my sternum, a sudden, urgent need to retreat. I mumbled a quick, "Excuse me," my voice sounding strangely thin, and immediately pivoted, walking with exaggerated purpose toward the relative safety of the garden, my pace too quick, my focus entirely on putting distance between us.
